preamble.summary: A wisdom psalm contrasting the fate of a powerful person who trusts in wealth and destructive speech with the righteous who trust in God's faithful love. The superscription connects it to Doeg the Edomite's betrayal of the priests at Nob. The psalm moves from accusation to the wicked person's downfall to the psalmist's confident trust.
preamble.remarkable: The psalm's central image is botanical: the wicked person is uprooted and torn from the land of the living (v. 7), while the psalmist is a flourishing olive tree in God's house (v. 10). The contrast is between rootedness and rootlessness — the wicked trusted in wealth (a portable asset) while the righteous is planted in God's presence (a fixed location). The olive tree metaphor is carefully chosen: olive trees live for centuries, produce fruit continually, and are nearly impossible to kill. Even when cut down, they regenerate from the roots. David is saying his trust in God's chesed has root systems that outlast destruction.
preamble.friction: The superscription's connection to Doeg the Edomite (1 Samuel 22:9-19) is not immediately obvious from the psalm's content, which addresses a more generalized 'mighty one' (gibbor). Doeg informed Saul that the priests at Nob had helped David, and then personally slaughtered 85 priests when Saul's own soldiers refused. The psalm may have originated from that specific event and been generalized for liturgical use. Hebrew verse numbering counts the two-verse superscription as verses 1-2.
preamble.connections: The Doeg episode is narrated in 1 Samuel 21:7 and 22:9-19. The olive tree image connects to Jeremiah 11:16, where Israel is called a 'green olive tree,' and Romans 11:17-24, where Paul develops olive tree theology. The tongue-as-weapon imagery (vv. 4-6) parallels Psalm 57:4 and James 3:5-8. The contrast between trusting in wealth and trusting in God anticipates Jesus's teaching in Luke 12:16-21.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, Maschil, A Psalm of David,
verses.rendering:
For the director of music.
A maskil of David.
translator_notes: maskil is a psalm classification appearing in 13 psalm superscriptions. Its exact meaning is debated — it may derive from sakal ('to be prudent, to have insight') and indicate a wisdom or instructional psalm. The term suggests this psalm is meant to teach something, not merely express emotion.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּב֤וֹא ׀ דּוֹאֵ֣ג הָאֲדֹמִי֮ וַיַּגֵּ֥ד לְשָׁא֗וּל וַיֹּ֥אמֶר ל֑וֹ בָּ֥א דָ֝וִ֗ד אֶל־בֵּ֥ית אֲחִימֶֽלֶךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: when Doeg the Edomite came and told Saul, and said unto him, David is come to the house of Ahimelech.
verses.rendering:
When Doeg the Edomite came and told Saul,
saying to him, "David has come to the house of Ahimelech."
translator_notes: Doeg was Saul's chief herdsman, an Edomite in Israelite service. His report to Saul about David's visit to the priest Ahimelech at Nob (1 Samuel 22:9-10) led to the massacre of 85 priests. Doeg personally carried out the slaughter when Saul's own soldiers refused. The psalm's focus on the destructive tongue makes sense in this context — Doeg's words killed an entire priestly community.

verses.text_hebrew: מַה־תִּתְהַלֵּ֣ל בְּ֭רָעָה הַגִּבּ֑וֹר חֶ֥סֶד אֵ֝֗ל כׇּל־הַיּֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: Why boastest thou thyself in mischief, O mighty man? the goodness of God endureth continually.
verses.rendering:
Why do you boast in evil, mighty one?
The faithful love of God endures all day long.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with a rhetorical question directed at the gibbor ('mighty one, warrior, powerful person') — a term that can be honorific but here drips with irony. This 'mighty one' boasts in ra'ah ('evil, calamity, harm'). The second line provides the devastating contrast: while the gibbor boasts in his capacity for destruction, chesed El ('the faithful love of God') persists kol ha-yom ('all day long, continually'). The juxtaposition is the psalm's thesis: destructive human power is temporary; God's loyal love is constant.
translator_notes: The syntax of verse 3b is compressed and debated. Some read it as a continuation of the question: 'Why do you boast in evil, mighty one — while the faithful love of God endures all day?' Others read it as a separate declaration. Either way, the contrast between human evil and divine chesed is the structural foundation of the psalm.

key_terms.semantic_range: mighty warrior, hero, champion, powerful person, strongman
key_terms.note: gibbor normally carries respect — it describes military heroes and is even applied to God (Isaiah 9:6). Here it is used sarcastically: this 'mighty one' achieves greatness through treachery and words, not genuine valor.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love, covenant faithfulness, kindness, mercy, devotion
key_terms.note: Set against the gibbor's evil, God's chesed provides the psalm's moral anchor. Whatever the powerful do, God's covenant loyalty endures without interruption.

verses.text_hebrew: הַוּ֥וֹת תַּחְשֹׁ֗ב לְ֫שׁוֹנֶ֥ךָ כְּתַ֥עַר מְ֝לֻטָּ֗שׁ עֹשֵׂ֥ה רְמִיָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy tongue deviseth mischiefs; like a sharp razor, working deceitfully.
verses.rendering:
Your tongue devises destruction,
like a sharpened razor, working deceit.
verses.expanded_rendering: The accusation focuses on the tongue — the organ of speech that Doeg used to inform on David. The tongue devises havvot ('destructions, ruin, calamity' — from the root havah, which gives us the name of Eve's lament). It is compared to a ta'ar melutrash ('sharpened razor') — not a sword or spear but a razor, an instrument designed for precise, close cutting. The image is surgical: this tongue does not bludgeon but slices with calculated precision. And it works remiyyah ('deceit, treachery') — the cutting is done under cover of something else. Doeg did not attack the priests directly at first; he reported facts to Saul, letting the king's paranoia do the work.
translator_notes: The razor metaphor is more chilling than a sword metaphor would be. A sword is an obvious weapon; a razor is a tool for grooming that has been repurposed for harm. The destruction comes from something that looks innocuous — a report, a piece of information, a truth told at the right moment to the right person.

verses.text_hebrew: אָהַ֣בְתָּ רָּ֣ע מִטּ֑וֹב שֶׁ֓קֶר ׀ מִדַּבֵּ֖ר צֶ֣דֶק סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou lovest evil more than good; and lying rather than to speak righteousness. Selah.
verses.rendering:
You love evil more than good,
lying more than speaking what is right.
Selah.
translator_notes: The comparison is not between evil and neutrality but between evil and good — this person actively prefers destruction over benefit, falsehood over truth. The selah at the end signals a musical pause, giving the accusation time to settle.

verses.text_kjv: Thou lovest all devouring words, O thou deceitful tongue.
verses.rendering:
You love every devouring word,
you deceitful tongue.
translator_notes: divrei vala ('devouring words') — vala means 'to swallow, to devour, to engulf.' These are words that consume people, that destroy lives when released. The phrase leshon mirmah ('tongue of deceit') becomes an epithet — the person is identified with their organ of destruction.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּם־אֵל֮ יִתָּצְךָ֢ לָ֫נֶ֥צַח יַחְתְּךָ֣ וְיִסָּחֲךָ֣ מֵאֹ֑הֶל וְשֵֽׁרֶשְׁךָ֙ מֵאֶ֖רֶץ חַיִּ֣ים סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: God shall likewise destroy thee for ever, he shall take thee away, and pluck thee out of thy dwelling place, and root thee out of the land of the living. Selah.
verses.rendering:
So God will tear you down forever;
he will snatch you and rip you from your tent,
and uproot you from the land of the living.
Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: Four verbs of destruction cascade in rapid succession: yittatsekha ('tear you down' — demolish, as a building is demolished), yachtekhah ('snatch you' — seize violently, grab like coals from a fire), yissachekhah me-ohel ('rip you from your tent' — uproot from your dwelling), and shereshkha me-erets chayyim ('uproot you from the land of the living' — the botanical metaphor of pulling out roots). The progression moves from structural destruction to violent seizure to uprooting — the mighty one will be treated like a weed pulled from the ground, roots and all. The phrase erets chayyim ('land of the living') means simply 'the world of the alive' — this person will be removed from existence entirely.
translator_notes: The four verbs create a sense of escalating violence. God does not merely punish; he demolishes, snatches, rips, and uproots. The language mirrors the violence the gibbor inflicted through speech — destruction returns to the destroyer in physical form.

verses.text_kjv: The righteous also shall see, and fear, and shall laugh at him:
verses.rendering:
The righteous will see and stand in awe,
and they will laugh at him:
translator_notes: The sequence — seeing, fearing, laughing — traces the emotional arc of watching divine justice unfold. The fear (yira'u) is not terror but awe at God's justice. The laughter (yischaku) is not cruelty but the release of tension when the bully finally falls. The righteous have lived under this person's threats; his downfall vindicates their endurance.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנֵּ֤ה הַגֶּ֗בֶר לֹ֤א יָשִׂ֥ים אֱלֹהִ֗ים מָֽ֫עוּזּ֥וֹ וַ֭יִּבְטַח בְּרֹ֣ב עׇשְׁר֑וֹ יָ֝עֹ֗ז בְּהַוָּתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Lo, this is the man that made not God his strength; but trusted in the abundance of his riches, and strengthened himself in his wickedness.
verses.rendering:
"Look — the man who would not make God his refuge,
but trusted in his great wealth
and grew strong in his own destruction."
verses.expanded_rendering: The righteous speak, diagnosing the fallen one's error. The word gever ('man, strong man') echoes gibbor from verse 3 — the mighty one is now just a man. His fundamental mistake: lo yasim Elohim ma'uzzo ('he did not make God his refuge'). Instead, he trusted (yivtach) in rov oshro ('the abundance of his wealth') and grew strong (ya'oz) in havvato ('his destruction/ruin'). The terrible irony is that the very thing the gibbor used as a weapon — destruction — became the thing he relied on. He strengthened himself through causing harm, and harm became his identity. When God uprooted him, there was nothing left.
translator_notes: The quotation marks in the rendering indicate that the righteous are speaking — this is their commentary on the fallen gibbor. The diagnosis is precise: the problem was not weakness but misplaced trust. Strength invested in wealth and destruction rather than in God produces a person who looks powerful but has no roots.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲנִ֤י ׀ כְּזַ֣יִת רַ֭עֲנָן בְּבֵ֣ית אֱלֹהִ֑ים בָּטַ֥חְתִּי בְחֶֽסֶד־אֱ֝לֹהִ֗ים עוֹלָ֥ם וָעֶֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: But I am like a green olive tree in the house of God: I trust in the mercy of God for ever and ever.
verses.rendering:
But I am like a flourishing olive tree
in the house of God.
I trust in the faithful love of God
forever and always.
verses.expanded_rendering: The contrast with the uprooted gibbor is absolute. David is a zayit ra'anan ('flourishing olive tree') — green, productive, deeply rooted, and planted be-veit Elohim ('in the house of God'). Olive trees are among the longest-lived trees in the Mediterranean world, producing fruit for centuries. They are also extraordinarily resilient — even when cut to a stump, they regenerate from the root system. The gibbor was uprooted; David is planted. The gibbor trusted in wealth; David trusts in chesed Elohim ('the faithful love of God'). And the time frame is olam va-ed ('forever and always') — a doubled expression of eternity that contrasts with the gibbor's permanent destruction (la-netsach, 'forever,' v. 7). Both fates are permanent, but they point in opposite directions.
translator_notes: Olive trees were planted in temple courtyards and palace grounds throughout the ancient Near East. The image of being 'in the house of God' as an olive tree suggests permanent residence in God's presence — a living thing rooted where God dwells.
The phrase olam va-ed ('forever and always') uses two words for permanence: olam ('age, eternity, ancient time') and ed ('perpetuity, continuing'). The doubling emphasizes duration without end.

verses.text_hebrew: אוֹדְךָ֣ לְ֭עוֹלָם כִּ֣י עָשִׂ֑יתָ וַאֲקַוֶּ֖ה שִׁמְךָ֥ כִּי־ט֝֗וֹב נֶ֣גֶד חֲסִידֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: I will praise thee for ever, because thou hast done it: and I will wait on thy name; for it is good before thy saints.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm closes with thanksgiving and patient hope. David will thank God le-olam ('forever') — matching the permanence of his trust in verse 10. The reason: ki asita ('because you have acted') — God has done something, though the psalm leaves the specific action unstated, trusting that God's track record speaks for itself. Then David will qavah ('wait, hope, look expectantly') for God's name — shem being God's reputation, character, and revealed identity. This waiting happens neged chasidekha ('in the presence of your faithful ones') — the community of those marked by chesed, the very quality that anchors the entire psalm. The gibbor stood alone in his wealth; David stands among the faithful.
translator_notes: chasidim ('faithful ones') derives from the same root as chesed. These are the people defined by covenant loyalty — they reflect God's character in human community. David's final word places him not in isolation but in the company of the faithful, contrasting with the gibbor's solitary fall.
